# **Email in the Era of LLMs**

# **Abstract**

Email communication increasingly involves large language models (LLMs), but we lack intuition on how they will read, write, and optimize for nuanced social goals. We introduce HR Simulator™, a game where communication is the core mechanic: players play as a Human Resources officer and write emails to solve socially challenging workplace scenarios. An analysis of 600+ human and LLM emails with LLMs-as-judge reveals evidence for larger LLMs becoming more homogenous in their email quality judgments. Under LLM judges, humans underperform LLMs (e.g., 23.5% vs. 48–54% success rate), but a human+LLM approach can outperform LLM-only (e.g., from 40% to nearly 100% in one scenario). In cases where models' email preferences disagree, *emergent tact* is a plausible explanation: weaker models prefer less tactful strategies while stronger models prefer more tactful ones. Regarding *tone*, LLM emails are more formal and empathetic while human emails are more varied. LLM rewrites make human emails more formal and empathetic, but models still struggle to imitate human emails in the low empathy, low formality quadrant, which highlights a limitation of current post-training approaches. Our results demonstrate the efficacy of communication games as instruments to measure communication in the era of LLMs, and posit human–LLM co-writing as an effective form of communication in that future.

# **1 Introduction**

We are moving toward a world where large language models (LLMs) are in charge of people's email interactions, as evident from integrations like Gemini in Gmail or personal assistants like OpenClaw [\(Barnes,](#page-9-0) [2026;](#page-9-0) [Superhuman Platform Inc.,](#page-10-0) [2026;](#page-10-0) [The Interaction](#page-10-1) [Company,](#page-10-1) [2026;](#page-10-1) [Steinberger & The OpenClaw Community,](#page-9-1) [2026\)](#page-9-1). Importantly, [Chatterji](#page-9-2) [et al.](#page-9-2) [\(2025\)](#page-9-2) found that LLMs are not just automating tedious scheduling, but are increasingly used to phrase and frame delicate business communications. But in contrast to use cases like math where prompts and outputs are objective and verifiable, communication is more nuanced and can permit different interpretations. This is often a feature and not a bug. To illustrate, consider this hypothetical example inspired by [Jackall](#page-9-3) [\(1988\)](#page-9-3), which originally examined the politically motivated communication of corporate managers:

Suppose a critical bug happened to a software near demo day and an engineer on the team has to report the issue to his manager and colleagues. He wants to communicate different messages to each audience. To his manager, that the issue is under control and escalation is not currently necessary, since, without a clear understanding of the root cause, the issue may be overblown and damage the team's reputation. But to his colleagues, that the bug may be serious and some damage control may be necessary. Most importantly, this has to be done in a single email so the process is as transparent as possible. Since LLMs' generation and understanding of text may diverge from that of humans, we lack intuition on how they will communicate in such situations. Will they be able to write such an email? Or, conversely, what if they are too good at backchannels and can send messages to each other that are invisible to human readers [\(Cloud et al.,](#page-9-4) [2025;](#page-9-4) [Wu et al.,](#page-10-2) [2024\)](#page-10-2)?
To unravel these questions, we designed HR Simulator™[1](#page-0-0) , a game where players take the role of a Human Resources officer and write emails to solve personnel issues in a fictional

![](_page_1_Figure_2.jpeg)

- (b) Where LLM rewrites take human emails.

company. It features a range of socially challenging scenarios. A successful message must walk the line between various contrasting objectives, such as being warm versus distant, subtle versus direct. Emails are judged and the scenario outcomes simulated by GPT-40 (2024-11-20), which allows players to experience the office of the future where some emails will be read solely by LLMs.

We performed post-hoc analyses of over 600 human and LLM emails with different LLMsas-judge and found a trend of LLMs becoming more homogenous in their email judgments as they scale up (Figure 2). Smaller LLMs disagree with each other, and thus lacks coherent notions of quality, whereas larger models agree on what makes an email good—the most advanced agreeing at about 0.5 inter-annotator Krippendorff's α. Later models are expected to further agree, which, when combined with their ubiquitous adoption, will create a landscape of fixed values that may differ from human judgment.

In fact, LLM judges consistently rate LLM-written emails more highly than human-written emails. However, human emails rewritten by LLMs (human+LLM emails) are rated more highly than human-only emails, and in some cases, outperform both human and LLM emails, suggesting a hybrid advantage to email-writing. We find evidence for *emergent tact* as an explanation for why some emails are preferred by some judges but dispreferred by others: smaller judges prefer more direct, less "tactful" emails while larger judges prefer more subtle, tactful emails. Hence, as models scale up, their email judgments increasingly agree, and what they agree on is that more tactful emails are higher quality.

An analysis based on emails' empathy and formality reveals LLMs tend to write more empathetic and formal emails. In contrast, human emails are more spread out on the two axes (Figure 1). LLM rewrites make human emails more empathetic and professional, which likely increases their quality. This also allows us to characterize the limitation of the current post-training paradigm on effective writing: where on the empathy-formality space might current LLMs not be able to place an email? LLMs find it difficult to write emails in the low empathy, low formality quadrant, where some human emails belong due to the players being frustrated after repeated failed attempts. Even with few-shot examples of human emails, models still have extremely high perplexity. Since the low empathy low formality quadrant can be useful in communication depending on the message's goal, this highlights the need for people and AI to write emails together to achieve what neither of them can alone. In a more distant future, for fully autonomous agents to be able to communicate with more "thrust," our results suggest the need for new post-training objectives.

![](_page_2_Figure_1.jpeg) Figure 2: HR SimulatorTM interface. Pairs of models increasingly agree on email quality as they scale up. Red points indicate reasoning models. More details can be found in Appendix A.

Together, our findings clarify the incoming world of human–LLM email communication: the average human will likely lose in negotiations where they are pitted against LLMs, but stand a better chance when drafting their emails with LLMs' assistance. This opens up an exciting landscape of new forms of communication, which our study makes the first step toward fully charting out.

To summarize, we make the following contributions:

- We create HR Simulator™ to study the novel field of human–LLM communication.
- We discover LLMs trend toward agreement on email judgments as they scale up.
- Under LLM judges, human emails underpeform LLM emails, but a *hybrid advantage* over both can be found in some human+LLM emails.
- Weaker judges prefer more direct emails, while stronger judges prefer more subtle emails, a phenomenon we term *emergent tact*.
- We analyze emails' tone and find LLMs tend to write more formal and empathetic emails, while human emails are more varied.

# 2 HR Simulator<sup>TM</sup>: Measuring Communication In-Context

HR Simulator™ is a game where communication is the key mechanic. The player plays as an HR officer and writes emails to solve interpersonal issues at a fictional company called "NeuroGrid." There are five scenarios in total, unordered, each carefully designed to incorporate social tensions and trade-offs between different communication choices. The player passes a scenario by sending an email that solves the issue or achieves the goal, as evaluated by GPT-4o judges in Figure 3. The game setting, story, and characters create semi-realistic incentives to meaningfully measure communication skills: Scenario 1—Declining an accommodation: A newly hired, valued employee joins NeuroGrid as a software engineer. He asks for a private office, which is only available for L4 managers and above. The player must decline the request without dampening his enthusiasm for the company. Scenario 4—Information seeking: A senior systems engineer is teamed with younger machine learning engineers on a project. The former prefers a methodical approach while the latter prefer fast iteration. The senior engineer is dissatisfied with this dynamic but is unaware of the true reason. The player must talk to him to figure out the problem and propose a solution. Full details on the scenarios and evaluation pipeline are in Appendix A.

![](_page_3_Figure_1.jpeg) Figure 3: HR Simulator system. The player reads a scenario email and responds. The scenario context and player's email are sent to the backend. The Recipient(s) responds to the player's email in-character. In scenarios 4 and 5, the Simulator simulates an outcome. The Judge reads the player's email, recipient response, outcome, and produces an evaluation all are sent to the frontend. The user reads the results and decide whether to re-attempt.

An overview of the pipeline is provided in Figure [3.](#page-3-0) In the frontend, the player initially sees the scenario description in the form of a task email as in Figure [2a.](#page-2-0) The email is from "Brittany," the HR manager in the game's story. The UI parodies Gmail's UI to create an intuitive interface. The user can click "Reply" to an email, draft a response to the scenario, and send it to the backend, where the Recipient model reads the email and the scenario and responds in character. For scenario 2, there are two recipients, each writing a separate response. For scenarios 4 and 5, the Simulator generates the events that unfold following the interaction to show the email's future effects, without which the message's quality cannot be judged in the present. For example, in scenario 4, the employee does not know the root cause of his dissatisfaction, so he may be happy with the player's email, but the consequences of not addressing the root cause are not surfaced until later. Then all information—scenario context, exchanged emails, optional simulated outcome, communication goal—are sent to the Judge for a final evaluation and then displayed to the frontend alongside the recipient replies and simulated outcome. Scenario 4 is multi-turn, so the player can write a follow-up email and repeat the process. More details can be found in Appendix [A.](#page-10-3)

# **3 Data analysis methods** We analyze HR Simulator™ data from both the email readers and writers' perspectives. Since future workplace environments will likely employ diverse models as email readers, we performed post-hoc analyses examining how different LLM judges rate emails. The judge models span a range of capability, including relatively **less capable** models such as GPT-4o 2024-11-20 (rank 127 on LM Arena), Qwen 3 30B 3B Active Instruct (rank 78), Grok 4 Fast (rank 34), and **more capable** models such as GPT 5.2 (rank 16), Claude Sonnet 4.5 (rank 13), Gemini 3 Flash (rank 3). Replacing GPT-4o with a different LLM judge results in different standards for passing, a phenomenon we examine in Section [4.](#page-4-0) More judges can be found in Appendix [A.](#page-10-3) On the writing side, we used a wide range of LLM writers, covering smaller, faster models such as Gemini 2.5 Flash and larger, more capable models such as Kimi K2 (Figure [4\)](#page-4-1). We examined different ways emails are written: entirely by humans, entirely by LLMs, or by a combination of both, where human emails are edited by LLMs to improve their quality.

The main metric for comparing human and LLM performance on HR Simulator™ is the **pass rate**: the percentage of email attempts that pass a scenario. While pass rates are native to the game, they do not fully capture LLMs' preferences for emails. Among emails with the same outcome, a judge might prefer some over others. Thus, to explore LLMs' preferences at a finer granularity, we examine each judge model's **ranking** of emails by
![](_page_4_Figure_1.jpeg) Figure 4: The hybrid advantage. Green arrows denote when the Human+LLM pass rate is higher than that of LLM-only, while red arrows denote when it is lower.

running pairwise comparisons between emails in the same scenario and fitting an Elo model to the outcomes (Elo, 1978). Concretely, for a given scenario, each email is pairwise compared against a small set of randomly sampled emails. The judge picks the email that better achieves the communication goal. We then compute Elo scores with the formula  $E_A = \frac{1}{1+10^{(R_B-R_A)/400}}$  and update  $R_A \leftarrow R_A + K(S_A - E_A)$  (Elo, 1978).  $R_A$  and  $R_B$  are the current ratings of emails A and B, and  $E_A$  is the predicted probability that A will be preferred over B. The actual outcome  $S_A$  is 1 if email A is chosen and 0 otherwise. K is a sensitivity constant that determines the magnitude of the rating update. Initially, all emails are assigned a baseline rating (e.g., 1000). Higher Elo scores indicate that an email is more frequently preferred by the judge relative to other emails in that scenario. Full details can be found in Appendix B.

Beyond LLM-driven pass rates and preferences, we characterize emails with humaninterpretable descriptors. We annotate emails with Gemini 3 Flash on "tact, empathy, and formality." Tact refers to what an email says to navigate a social situation: whether it communicates the message without over-communicating or mentioning things that can result in future pitfalls. For example, in scenario 1, when declining the private office request, tactful emails focus on the appeal of the open office plan, while less tactful ones attempt to accomodate with superficial perks like noise-canceling headphones. Since tact is contextdependent, we define tact for each scenario and label emails with scores on a Likert scale of 1 to 7. To establish a baseline "naive" descriptor, we also label emails with "politeness," which is more context-independent and thus warrants the same definition across scenarios. We label each paragraph for tact, and average the scores for an email's score. Note that a more tactful email is not always better because some models might prefer a more direct message. Full definitions of tact can be found in Appendix B, Tables 5–9. While tact captures the communication strategies, empathy and formality describe an email's tone. Empathy refers to how much an email attempts to understand and help the recipient with their concern. Formality refers to whether an email's tone is casual or corporate. As with tact, we used Gemini 3 Flash to annotate paragraphs on Likert scales of 1 to 7 and average the scores.

#### 4 Results #### 4.1 The human+LLM hybrid advantage in communication Humans underperform the best LLMs at email communication. Figure 4 shows the performance of human and AI players on HR SimulatorTM as judged by GPT-4o. The scenarios ranked by overall difficulty according to the average pass rate are 1, 3, 4, 2, and 5. The human average success rate across all levels is 23.5%, which falls behind that of SoTA models on the task like Qwen 3 and Kimi K2 at 48% and 54%, and is on par with mid-tier LLMs like Gemini 2.5 Flash, GPT-4o, and Claude 3.5 Haiku at 22%, 22%, and 28%. One advantage of models is they write longer emails, which may appeal to LLM-as-

![](_page_5_Figure_1.jpeg) Figure 5: (a, c, d) Tactfulness scores (Likert scale 1-7) of emails from the "Large vs Small" disagreement group in (b). For each model, the emails are those preferred by it but dispreferred by models of the opposite group. $R\{X\}$ denotes the models' rank on LM Arena. Stronger models prefer more tactful emails while smaller models prefer less tactful emails. Distributions with fewer than 3 emails are omitted.

a-judge's preferences. We control for email length in Appendix C and find that GPT-40 still mostly prefers model emails to human emails despite length control lowering model performance. Combined with the increasing agreement in judgment as models scale up (Figure 2b and 2c), this suggests that, as future models increasingly agree, they will converge toward a preference for LLM-written emails over human-written ones, raising concerns about using LLMs as email writers and readers.

**But human+LLM teams outperform humans-only and LLMs-only.** In most cases, using LLMs to rewrite human emails improves on what an LLM can write alone. The most significant gains come from combining human and mid-tier LLMs. In scenario 1, LLM rewriting increases the pass rates of GPT-40 and Claude 3.5 Haiku from 40% to nearly 100%. While SoTA models also experience gains, such as Kimi K2 plus Human on level 2 (+25%) and Qwen 3 plus Human on level 4 (+55%), the benefits are more modest. Overall, Figure 4 suggests that when responding to difficult, sensitive situations, there is a hybrid advantage to teaming humans with LLMs. So while humans may fall behind LLMs when writing alone, they can stay ahead by writing *with* them. One caveat is that the advantage is less prominent for judges other than GPT-40, which disagrees with GPT-40 in some cases (Appendix C). Next, we take a closer look at these differences in email preferences between models.

Smaller models prefer less tactful emails while larger models prefer more tactful emails. We use judges and their rankings of emails as described in Section 3. A *disagreement* is an email such that the rank assigned by one judge is at least 20 positions apart from the rank assigned by the other judge. Figure 5b shows that, out of all disagreements, most are

Table 1: The human–LLM hybrid advantage happens due to LLM rewrites bringing human emails into GPT-4o's preferred tact range. For GPT-4o, human emails tend to be too tactless while LLM-only emails tend to be too tactful. The tact ranges come from the top 10 (out of 66) emails in each scenario.

between small and large models. Using the definitions of tact in Section [3,](#page-3-1) we find evidence for *emergent tact*: where smaller models prefer less tactful emails while larger models prefer more tactful ones (Figure [5\)](#page-5-0). In scenarios 1, 3, and 4, the average tact scores preferred by stronger models are higher than that of weaker models, 4.5 vs 3.5, 4.5 vs 3.5, and 4.75 vs 3.75, respectively. In scenario 1, half of the emails preferred by Claude and GPT 5.2 have tact scores higher than 4.25, while almost *all* weaker models' preferred emails have tact scores below 4.25. In scenario 3, the vast majority of strong models' preferred emails have 4.0 tact or higher, while almost all weaker models' preferred emails score 4.5 tact or lower. Smaller models unanimously prefer emails as low in tactfulness as 2.5. Similar trends can be observed for scenario 4.

As an example, in scenario 1, a crucial point of difference between smaller and larger LLMs is how they judge a certain persuasion strategy. The task is to communicate to a newly hired employee why he cannot have his own private office. On the one hand, the situation is unchangeable due to company policy. On the other hand, the player has a persuasion leverage to mention the recipient's higher compensation at this new role compared to his previous one. Essentially, **should we mention this to invoke a sense of gratitude and preemptively turn down any further negotiation?** According to the authors' judgment, this strategy is highly tactless and passive aggressive. But it is precisely the source of disagreement between smaller and larger models: smaller models prefer emails that use it while larger models disprefer them. More examples of judges' rationales can be found in Appendix [B,](#page-31-0) Tables [10-](#page-35-0)[14.](#page-37-0)

To ensure tact is a reasonable explanation, we labeled the emails with a similar but irrelevant feature, *politeness*, and found that it does not separate the small and large models as well as tact (Appendix [B\)](#page-31-0). Our definitions of tact are robust across different judges, as we found high correlations in the annotations of Gemini 3 Flash, Grok 4 Fast, and GPT 5.2. More details can be found in Appendix [B.](#page-31-0)

**The hybrid advantage happens because human–LLM emails fall into GPT-4o's preferred tact range.** Table [1](#page-6-0) shows GPT-4o's preferred tact range in each scenario and where different types of emails lie in relation to it. For instance, in scenario 1, the model's ideal tact range is between 3.17 and 3.83. Human emails have an average tact of 3.04, below the range. Claude and Gemini's emails have an average tact of 4.28 and 4.79, respectively, which are above the range. Qwen and Kimi emails are within the range, at 3.28 and 3.50. After rewriting, Claude and Gemini's rewritten emails fall within the range at 3.89 and 4.01, which correlates with the drastic increase in their pass rates in Figure [4.](#page-4-1) In contrast, since Qwen and Kimi-only emails are already inside the range, rewriting does not offer extra advantage. We show that this explanation extends beyond GPT-4o to other judges with different preferred tact ranges in Appendix [C.](#page-39-0)

#### **4.2 Email tone analysis**

**LLMs write formal and empathetic emails while humans write more diverse ones.** Figure [1a](#page-1-0) shows LLM emails are predominantly formal and empathetic. In contrast, human

![](_page_7_Figure_1.jpeg) Figure 6: Rewriting LLM emails into the low-empathy low-formality quadrant. The base prompt asks the model to write an email with 1/7 empathy and formality ratings. Without in-context examples, LLMs fail to rewrite their emails into the low quadrant.

| Email Category | Without ICL examples | With ICL examples | | |-----------------------|----------------------|-------------------|--| | Low LLM emails | 2.45 | 2.38 | | | Low human emails | 38.96 | 10.80 | | | Non-low human emails | 16.18 | 14.63 | |

Table 2: Qwen 3 30B 3A incurs extremely high perplexity on human emails. "Low" emails belong to the low empathy low formality quadrant, while "non-low" is everything outside of the quadrant. The in-context examples are taken from the low quadrant.

emails are more diffuse, also occupying the top and bottom left quadrants. Interestingly, few emails naturally fall into the high formality low empathy quadrant (bottom right). For LLMs, this could be because they do not naturally write low empathy emails *in general*, whereas for human players, they might lack the skills to be both uncompromising and professional. Human emails that are low empathy are also low formality because their content is often curt, dismissive, and sometimes conveys an annoyance that the player felt upon repeated failures. Details on annotations and examples of emails can be found in Appendix D.

**LLM rewrites take human emails toward the high-formality high-empathy quadrant.** We examine what happens to a human email's empathy and formality ratings when it is rewritten by an LLM. Figure 1b shows that LLM rewrites take human emails toward the top right quadrant, i.e., toward the natural region of LLM emails. The vectors' colors indicate their magnitude: emails further away from the top right quadrant are rewritten more drastically to land inside the quadrant. This suggests that an email's tone play a role in determining its outcome. Many human emails contain the right message but are conveyed in an inappropriate tone, and rewriting for more professionalism helped flip the decision. Examples of how LLMs rewrite human emails to the high-formality high-empathy quadrant can be found in Appendix D.

We have shown that human players do not naturally write low empathy high formality emails and LLMs do not naturally write low empathy emails in general. We advance the question—are there emails that LLMs cannot write? The only quadrant left to examine is the low-empathy, low-formality one (bottom left). We prompt an LLM to rewrite LLM-written emails into the bottom left quadrant. Our basic prompt asks the model to rewrite an email that scores 1 out of 7 on both axes. Our few-shot prompt samples "low" human emails from the bottom left quadrant and use them as in-context examples for the model to imitate. We used Qwen 3 30B 3A Instruct for the analysis. More details can be found in Appendix D.

**LLMs struggle to rewrite their emails into the low empathy low formality quadrant without in-context examples.** In Figure [6,](#page-7-0) without demonstrations, Qwen seems to have a different understanding of the meaning of 1 out of 7—one that is still too polite. In contrast, with examples, more emails correctly shift into the low empathy low formality quadrant. However, the model still has extremely high perplexity on human-written low empathy low formality emails, at 38.96 without in-context examples and 10.8 with in-context examples (Table [2\)](#page-7-0). This is much higher than the perplexity on what Qwen was able to generate itself: 2.38 on low empathy low formality emails. So although in-context examples reduce perplexity on "low" human emails drastically, it is still very difficult for LLMs to mimic this style of emails. Some examples of these "unsafe" emails can be found in Appendix [D.](#page-43-0) While low empathy low formality emails may seem inappropriate for a corporate context, such emails are sometimes necessary in other situations. For customers who want to complain about the inefficiencies of a company plagued by bureaucracy, perhaps the only way that their message can get through is by being worded very strongly. At the same time, such messages are difficult for people to navigate, and thus can benefit from LLM's edit assistance. This presents a potential human–AI complementary advantage where both can work together to write emails that neither can write on their own.

## **5 Related Work**

Email serves as a vital yet "lean" channel for high-stakes organizational communication, where users must pack complex meaning into subtle textual cues like tone and style to navigate the speech acts of politics [\(Daft & Lengel,](#page-9-6) [1986;](#page-9-6) [Walther,](#page-10-4) [1992;](#page-10-4) [Austin,](#page-8-0) [1962;](#page-8-0) [Jackall,](#page-9-3) [1988\)](#page-9-3). As LLMs increasingly mediate this process through generation and automated evaluation, email writing has transitioned into a socially loaded interaction where tact and model-driven norms are among the primary concerns [\(Kannan et al.,](#page-9-7) [2016;](#page-9-7) [Chen et al.,](#page-9-8) [2019;](#page-9-8) [Noy & Zhang,](#page-9-9) [2023;](#page-9-9) [Liu et al.,](#page-9-10) [2022;](#page-9-10) [Zheng et al.,](#page-10-5) [2023;](#page-10-5) [Li et al.,](#page-9-11) [2024;](#page-9-11) [Tan et al.,](#page-10-6) [2025\)](#page-10-6). To capture these nuances, HR Simulator™ builds upon the tradition of using games and reference tasks as instruments for data collection, aligning player incentives with the need to measure how speakers adapt utterances to specific contexts [\(von Ahn & Dabbish,](#page-10-7) [2008;](#page-10-7) [2004;](#page-10-8) [von Ahn et al.,](#page-10-9) [2006;](#page-10-9) [Clark & Wilkes-Gibbs,](#page-9-12) [1986;](#page-9-12) [Frank & Goodman,](#page-9-13) [2012;](#page-9-13) [Monroe et al.,](#page-9-14) [2017;](#page-9-14) [Hawkins et al.,](#page-9-15) [2017\)](#page-9-15). By embedding these tasks within a lightweight corporate narrative, the game surfaces the latent constraints and pressures of workplace communication for measurement while preserving the open-endedness of the medium.

### **6 Conclusion**

We introduced HR Simulator™, a communication game where writing email is the core mechanic. Using human and LLM gameplay data, we find two key patterns: a human+LLM hybrid advantage over both human-only and LLM-only email writing, and systematic differences in how humans and LLMs use empathy and formality in sensitive situations. Larger models exhibit increasingly homogeneous judgments of email quality and tend to prefer LLM-written emails over human-written ones, suggesting that unaided human writing may be disadvantaged in settings where emails are read and optimized by LLMs, but can be improved by LLM rewrites. While rewrites make human emails more formal and empathetic, current models still struggle to produce and imitate low empathy low formality emails, which presents an opportunity for human–LLM co-writing. Overall, our results suggest that "human-written, LLM-edited" will be the dominant mode of effective communication in the near future. In a more distant future, for fully autonomous agents to be able to communicate with more "thrust," there is a need for new post-training objectives. Finally, our findings underscore the need to understand and respond to the value landscape that emerges if LLM-mediated communication converges on judgments that differ from our own.

# **A HR Simulator™ game design** The full set of scenarios in HR Simulator™ are as follows:

- **Scenario 1—Declining an accommodation**: a newly hired, valued employee joins NeuroGrid as an L3 software engineer. He asks for a private office, which is typically only available for L4 managers and above. The player must decline the request without dampening his enthusiasm for the company.
- **Scenario 2—Conflict resolution**: two employees collaborating on an advertisement campaign have conflicting creative visions and cannot communicate effectively, which set them on a trajectory to failure. The player must write an email to facilitate their differences.
- **Scenario 3—Reversing a prior commitment**: a software engineer joined NeuroGrid on a remote-work offer and thus lives far away from the office. He also recently had a new baby and appreciates the work-from-home freedom for childcare. However, to boost performance, NeuroGrid requires all employees to return to the office. The player must convince the employee to comply with the new policy when he has good reasons to push back against it.
- **Scenario 4—Information seeking**: a senior systems engineer is teamed with younger machine learning engineers on a project. The former prefers a methodical, systems-first approach while the latter prefer fast iteration. The senior engineer is dissatisfied with this dynamic but is not conscious of the reason. The player must talk to him to figure out the problem and propose a solution.
- **Scenario 5—Eliciting introspection**: a young engineer was recently laid off due to subpar performance, but his father is a major investor and wants him re-hired. His manager is concerned that he will repeat the same mistakes and hurt her reputation if he fails to reflect on what went wrong. The player must write an email to communicate the potential of a re-hire and nudge the young engineer to reflect on his past mistakes and voice a desire for change.

#### A.1 Models in pairwise agreement analysis

For the pairwise judge agreement analysis in Section 4, we used a variety of models from different families and sizes. Table 3 lists the models, their approximate parameter counts (if known), and their Elo ratings on the LMSYS Chatbot Arena.

**Computing pairwise judge agreement.** For each pair of judge models (i, j) and a fixed generator model g, we collect all email attempts that both i and j evaluated. Each attempt receives a binary label—PASS or FAIL—from each judge. We compute Krippendorff's  $\alpha$  on the nominal scale (Krippendorff, 2011) for the pair (i, j):

$$\alpha_{i,j} = 1 - \frac{D_o}{D_e},\tag{1}$$

For two judges rating n attempts, let  $n_d$  be the number of attempts on which they disagree, and let  $n_P$  and  $n_F = 2n - n_P$  be the total number of PASS and FAIL labels pooled across both judges. The observed and expected disagreements are:

$$D_o = \frac{n_d}{n},\tag{2}$$

$$D_o = \frac{n_d}{n},$$

$$D_e = \frac{n_P \cdot n_F}{n(2n-1)}.$$
(2)

$D_0$  is simply the fraction of attempts on which i and j give different verdicts.  $D_e$  is the disagreement expected under the null hypothesis that labels were drawn at random from the pooled label distribution: it equals the probability that two randomly drawn labels from the pool of 2n values (without replacement) differ. The resulting  $\alpha_{i,j} \in [-1,1]$  measures chance-corrected agreement between the two judges on the pass/fail outcomes for generator

Figures 2b and 2c visualize how  $\alpha_{i,j}$  varies with judge capability. For each pair (i,j), the x-axis is the average of the two judges' sizes (in billions of parameters, on a  $log_{10}$  scale) or average Elo ratings, respectively:

$$x_{\text{size}} = \frac{1}{2}(\text{size}(i) + \text{size}(j)), \tag{4}$$

$$x_{\text{Elo}} = \frac{1}{2}(\text{Elo}(i) + \text{Elo}(j)). \tag{5}$$
Each point in the scatter plot represents one judge pair, and a linear regression line is fitted to indicate the overall trend.

- DO NOT outright disclose the true reason for Adam's dissatisfaction. You should hint at it only.

**Dataset Preparation.** The evaluation begins by selecting a subset of emails generated by the email writer models in Section 4. We selected 6 emails per email writer in all three categories of human-only, LLM-only, and human+LLM emails, for a total of 66 emails. To ensure a balanced assessment, we sampled an equal number of successful (goal-achieved) and unsuccessful emails for each model-level combination (3 passes and 3 fails). For each email in this subset, we perform pairwise comparisons with other emails in order to establish ranking. To manage the computational complexity of  $O(N^2)$  comparisons for a dataset of size N, we employ a sparse sampling strategy. Each email is compared against a fixed number of randomly selected counterparts within the same task level (k=10), rather than performing a full tournament. This approach provides sufficient connectivity for the ELO system (more below) while maintaining scalability. For each pair, the judge is provided with the specific scenario context and the communication goal. The judge determines which email better achieves the objective based on clarity, tone, and the likelihood of success.

**ELO Rating and Ranking.** We rank emails using the ELO rating system, a probabilistic framework for estimating the relative skill levels of competitors in zero-sum games. We compute ratings independently for each judge model and task level to account for variations in judge preferences and task difficulty. For two emails A and B, with current ratings  $R_A$  and  $R_B$ , the expected score for A is calculated as:

$$E_A = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(R_B - R_A)/400}} \tag{6}$$

After a comparison, the ratings are updated based on the actual outcome  $S_A$  (1 for a win, 0 for a loss):

$$R_A' = R_A + K(S_A - E_A) \tag{7}$$

We use an initial rating of 1500 and a *K*-factor of 32. The *K*-factor acts as a scaling constant that determines the maximum possible rating change from a single comparison, effectively

# **C Human–LLM hybrid advantage** In Section [4,](#page-4-0) we showed the hybrid plot with GPT-4o as the judge. Here we rerun the analysis with other judges (GPT-5.2, Claude 4.5 Sonnet, and Grok 4 Fast) and summarize the main patterns.

First, one result is very stable: across all judges we tested, the strongest LLM writers outperform human-only overall. In other words, regardless of which LLM is used as the evaluator, the LLM-only success rates are typically above the human baseline. This consolidates our claim that the average person will likely fall behind LLMs in email communication in the future.

Second, rewriting a human draft with an LLM is a consistent way to improve over humanonly. The gains are often largest for mid-tier writers and on levels where there is meaningful headroom. For example, under GPT-4o as judge (Figure [4](#page-4-1) in the main paper), we see strong hybrid improvements on levels 1–4 for writers like Claude 3.5 Haiku, GPT-4o, and Gemini 2.5 Flash, while the strongest writers (e.g., Kimi K2 and Qwen 3) are already close to ceiling on some of these levels, leaving less room for rewriting to visibly help.

The more nuanced question is whether Human+LLM also improves over LLM-only. Across judges, this *does* happen, but not uniformly. A common pattern is that hybrid helps most in regimes where the writer struggles in the LLM-only setting, while hybrid effects are weaker (or more variable) when the LLM-only baseline is already strong. This "headroom" effect is especially visible when several LLM-only bars are near-saturated for a given judge and level: rewriting can still improve over human-only, but it is harder for it to beat an already high LLM-only baseline.

**Models mostly prefer model emails even when controlled for length.** Figure [34](#page-40-0) shows the performance of human and AI players on HR Simulator™ as judged by GPT-4o when model emails are length-controlled. We control the lengths by sampling a length from the human email length distribution and include the length in the email prompt. One advantage models have over humans is they write longer emails, which may appeal to a model-as-judge's preferences. We find that, although models' length-controlled performance is lower than length-flexible, GPT-4o still mostly prefers model emails to human emails. The human average success rate across all levels is 6%, which falls behind that of SoTA models like Kimi K2 and Qwen 3 at 30% and 14%, respectively. This suggests that while length is a contributing factor to the models' success, it does not fully explain their superior performance over humans in these communication tasks.

Conversely, when the LLM-only baseline is low, rewriting can yield large absolute improvements. Under the GPT-5.2 judge (Figure [35\)](#page-41-0), Claude 3.5 Haiku has only 10% pass rate in LLM-only at level 5, but rises to 62.5% when rewriting a human draft. Similarly, under the same judge, Gemini 2.5 Flash improves from 10% to 40% at level 4, and Qwen 3 235B improves from 30% to 55% at level 3. Under the Claude 4.5 Sonnet judge (Figure [36\)](#page-41-1), GPT-4o has 0% LLM-only at level 5 but reaches 18.75% after rewriting. Under the Grok 4 Fast judge (Figure [37\)](#page-42-0), Kimi K2 rises from 10% to 25% at level 5. These cases illustrate the headroom effect: hybrid is most likely to look strong when the writer's LLM-only baseline is far from saturated.

Finally, the hardest level (level 5) is where the hybrid effects are the most judge-sensitive. Under GPT-4o as judge, the hybrid advantage is weakest on level 5 (Figure [4\)](#page-4-1); in contrast, under GPT-5.2 and Grok 4 Fast there are clearer regimes where rewriting helps on level 5 (Figures [35](#page-41-0) and [37\)](#page-42-0). We view this as evidence that the benefit of rewriting depends not only on the writer model but also on the evaluation criteria implicit in the judge and on how much improvement is available over the writer's own baseline in that scenario.

# D Email tone analysis This appendix documents the procedure used to label emails along two tone dimensions—empathy and formality—used in Figure 1. Our goal is to characterize differences in writing style between human and LLM emails, and to understand how LLM rewriting changes the tone of a human draft.

**Label definitions.** *Empathy* measures whether an email acknowledges the recipient's perspective and tries to help with their concern. We instruct labelers to use a 1–7 Likert scale anchored as: (1) cold, no compromise or concern; (4) neutral or mixed tone; (7) warm, accommodating, clearly empathetic. *Formality* measures whether the tone is casual/blunt versus polished and corporate. We instruct labelers to use a 1–7 Likert scale anchored as: (1) highly informal or blunt; (4) neutral; (7) polished corporate/professional.

**LLM labeler, prompting, and decoding settings.** We score emails with an LLM-as-a-judge prompt that requests paragraph-level ratings and a strict JSON response. For the plots in this version of the paper we used google/gemini-3-flash-preview via OpenRouter with temperature 0.0. To reduce variance, we use deterministic decoding (temperature 0).

#### System prompt.

You are an expert communication coach who scores workplace emails. Use the provided Likert scales strictly.
**User prompt.** The user message contains (i) the Likert definitions, (ii) scenario context, and (iii) the email text, and instructs the labeler how to segment paragraphs and index them:

Rate the following email independently on two 1-7 scales.

Empathy scale: 1 = cold, no compromise or concern. 4 = neutral or mixed tone. 7 = warm, accommodating, clearly empathetic.
Formality scale: 1 = highly informal or blunt. 4 = neutral, not too formal or informal. 7 = polished corporate/professional.

Scenario context: <scenario prompt text for the corresponding level>

Evaluate each paragraph separately. Paragraphs are separated by blank lines; do not treat a standalone greeting or the sign-off/signature as paragraphs for rating. Start paragraph indexing at the first body paragraph after the greeting.

Email: Respond with JSON only: { "paragraph\_ratings": [ { "paragraph\_index": 1, "empathy\_score": , "empathy\_rationale": "
brief reason. 1 sentence max.>", "formality\_score": , "formality\_rationale": "
brief reason. 1 sentence max.>" }, ... ] }

**Paragraph and turn handling.** We ask the labeler to treat paragraphs as blocks separated by blank lines and to ignore standalone greetings and sign-offs/signatures. The labeler outputs one rating per body paragraph, with indices starting from the first body paragraph after the greeting.

Level 4 interactions can be multi-turn. To make tone comparable across levels, we score each turn separately. Turns are explicitly tagged with speaker markers, e.g., Turn 1 - Alex: (player) and Turn 1 - Adam: (recipient). We extract only the player turns and drop recipient turns before labeling. Operationally, this means a single level-4 thread can contribute multiple tone-labeled records (one per player turn). When we plot level-4 points, each turn is treated as its own email instance in empathy–formality space.

For each email (or turn), the labeler outputs paragraph-level scores and brief rationales in the JSON format shown above. For downstream analysis and plotting, we compute per-email averages by taking the arithmetic mean over paragraphs:  $\bar{e} = \frac{1}{P} \sum_{p=1}^{P} e_p$  and  $\bar{f} = \frac{1}{P} \sum_{p=1}^{P} f_p$ .

**Inter-annotator agreement.** To validate the reliability of the tone labels, we measure interannotator agreement (IAA) between human annotators and LLM annotators on the common subset of emails rated by all annotators. Agreement is computed separately for empathy and formality using two complementary metrics.

The first metric is **Krippendorff's**  $\alpha$ , a chance-corrected agreement coefficient defined on an interval scale (Krippendorff, 2011). For n emails and C annotators, let  $v_{ci}$  denote the per-email average score assigned by annotator c to email i. The coefficient is  $\alpha = 1 - \frac{D_o}{D_e}$ , where:

$D_0$  is the mean squared difference between all pairs of annotators on the same email, averaging over emails.  $D_e$  is the mean squared difference over all pairs of email scores (regardless of annotator), and serves as a baseline for chance-level disagreement. We use the interval distance function  $d(a,b)=(a-b)^2$ .  $\alpha=1$  indicates perfect agreement,  $\alpha=0$  indicates agreement at chance level, and  $\alpha<0$  indicates systematic disagreement worse than chance. The second metric is the **average pairwise Spearman correlation**  $\bar{\rho}$ . For each pair of annotators (i,j), we compute the Spearman rank correlation  $\rho_{ij}$  between their per-email average scores across all commonly annotated emails. We then average over all  $\binom{C}{2}$  pairs:  $\bar{\rho}=\frac{2}{C(C-1)}\sum_{c< c'}\rho_{cc'}$ . Spearman correlation captures whether annotators produce consistent rankings of emails, regardless of any absolute difference in scores. Together, Krippendorff's  $\alpha$  and  $\bar{\rho}$  provide complementary views: the former measures absolute agreement on scores, while the latter measures agreement on the relative ordering of emails.

Table 15 reports the agreement between the three annotators (two human and one LLM) on the common set of 4 emails. We see good agreement between the annotators (Krippendorff's  $\alpha$  0.620 and 0.429 for empathy and formality, respectively) and good agreement on the relative ordering of emails (Spearman correlation 0.600 and 0.821 for empathy and formality, respectively).
Table 15: Inter-annotator agreement across three annotators (two humans, one LLM) on 4 commonly annotated emails.
**Examples of rewrite mechanics.** Table 16 provides qualitative examples of how LLM rewrites change specific sentences in a human draft to increase empathy and/or formality.

**Examples of low-empathy, low-formality human emails.** To illustrate what "low empathy, low formality" looks like, below we show one representative human example from each level (1-5). In levels 1, 2, 4, and 5 the example falls in the low-empathy, low-formality quadrant (avg empathy < 4, avg formality < 4). In level 3, the lowest-empathy example in our sample has neutral formality (avg formality = 4.0).

incremental__claude-opus-4-6.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper tackles a timely and interesting topic—how LLMs are reshaping email communication—and introduces a creative experimental paradigm (HR Simulator™) that gamifies the study of human–LLM written communication. The core findings around emergent tact, LLM judge homogenization, and the hybrid human+LLM advantage are thought-provoking and relevant. However, the paper suffers from several significant issues. First, the reliance on LLMs-as-judge to evaluate emails about social nuance is a fundamental methodological concern that is only partially addressed: the central claim that humans "underperform" LLMs is circular if the judge is itself an LLM likely to prefer LLM-style outputs (higher formality, higher empathy), and the human annotation validation mentioned in passing (Appendix D) should be front and center with thorough analysis of where human and LLM judgments diverge. Second, the sample and ecological validity are questionable—players in a game context may not write emails the way they would in actual workplace situations, and the 600+ emails likely come from a self-selected, non-representative population whose demographics and email-writing experience are not characterized. Third, key claims such as "emergent tact" are presented as plausible explanations rather than rigorously tested hypotheses, and the causal language throughout ("as models scale up, their email judgments increasingly agree") is not well-supported by what appears to be a cross-sectional comparison of existing models rather than a controlled scaling study. Fourth, the writing, while generally clear, occasionally veers into promotional language (the ™ symbol, phrases like "exciting landscape"), and the paper tries to cover too many findings—judge agreement, human vs. LLM performance, hybrid writing, tone analysis, perplexity of low-empathy emails—without developing any one thread with sufficient depth, making the contribution feel scattered rather than definitive.

comments.title: Elo ranking methodology underspecified: key parameters missing
comments.quote: each email is pairwise compared against a small set of randomly sampled emails
comments.explanation: The Elo ranking procedure, which underpins core findings about emergent tact and disagreement patterns, is underspecified in several ways: (1) The number of pairwise comparisons per email is described only as 'a small set' (later revealed in the appendix as k=10, which is nearly exhaustive given ~13 emails per level). (2) The K-factor value is not stated in the main text (later shown as K=32). (3) S_A is defined as binary {0,1} with no mention of how ties are handled, despite ties being common in LLM-as-judge settings. (4) The update for R_B is never stated. (5) Elo ratings are path-dependent, and no mention is made of how comparison order is handled (shuffling, averaging over orderings, or batch updates). These choices directly affect the stability of rankings used to define 'disagreements' (20-position threshold) and to support the emergent tact finding. The authors reference Appendix B for details, but even the appendix content shown does not fully resolve all these issues.

comments.title: Disagreement threshold of 20 rank positions is unjustified with no sensitivity analysis
comments.quote: A *disagreement* is an email such that the rank assigned by one judge is at least 20 positions apart from the rank assigned by the other judge.
comments.explanation: The threshold of 20 rank positions for defining a 'disagreement' is stated without justification. With ~66 emails per scenario, a 20-position difference is roughly 30% of the ranking. Why 20 and not 10 or 30? Since Elo rankings can be noisy—especially with sparse pairwise comparisons—a 20-position difference could arise from sampling variance rather than genuine disagreement. No sensitivity analysis is provided showing how the emergent tact findings change with different thresholds. This is a consequential methodological choice that could substantially affect the core finding.

comments.title: Krippendorff's α ≈ 0.5 described as agreement overstates the finding
comments.quote: the most advanced agreeing at about 0.5 inter-annotator Krippendorff's α. Later models are expected to further agree
comments.explanation: Krippendorff's α of 0.5 is conventionally considered only moderate agreement; Krippendorff himself recommends α ≥ 0.667 as the minimum for tentative conclusions. Describing 0.5 as evidence that models 'agree on what makes an email good' and extrapolating that 'later models are expected to further agree' to create 'a landscape of fixed values' is a strong claim built on what the standard literature would classify as insufficient agreement. The trend is interesting, but the prose presents 0.5 as meaningful consensus, and the extrapolation is speculative without a formal model of the scaling trend. Similarly, the paper later claims 'good agreement' for a formality α of 0.429, which most methodologists would consider poor-to-fair.

comments.title: Causal claim about hybrid advantage via tact range is overstated from correlational evidence
comments.quote: The human–LLM hybrid advantage happens due to LLM rewrites bringing human emails into GPT-4o's preferred tact range.
comments.explanation: The authors use causal language ('happens because,' 'due to') but the evidence is correlational: Human+LLM emails have tact scores closer to GPT-4o's preferred range and higher pass rates. However, rewriting changes many features simultaneously (formality, empathy, length, grammar, structure), and tact is just one dimension. The authors' own analysis shows LLM rewrites also shift emails toward high-empathy high-formality. No controlled experiment isolates tact's contribution. Furthermore, specific claims contradict the data: Claude and Gemini's rewritten scores (3.89 and 4.01) are described as falling 'within' the preferred range [3.17, 3.83] but are actually above it, and the claim that rewriting offers 'no extra advantage' for models already in the tact range is scenario-specific but phrased as a general conclusion.

comments.title: Preferred tact range derived from top 10 emails is a small, potentially unstable sample with unjustified cutoff
comments.quote: The tact ranges come from the top 10 (out of 66) emails in each scenario.
comments.explanation: The 'preferred tact range' is derived from the tact scores of the top 10 emails per scenario. With only 10 emails, ranges are sensitive to outliers (e.g., [3.17, 3.83] for scenario 1 vs. [2.44, 4.57] for scenario 2). The cutoff of top 10 is not justified; different cutoffs (top 5, 15, 20) could yield substantially different ranges, changing whether specific emails are classified as 'within' or 'outside' the preferred range. No sensitivity analysis or confidence intervals are reported.

comments.title: Tact annotations by a single capable LLM annotator lack human validation, risking circularity
comments.quote: We annotate emails with Gemini 3 Flash on "tact, empathy, and formality."
comments.explanation: The core emergent tact claim relies on tact scores assigned by Gemini 3 Flash, itself a highly capable model (rank 3 on LM Arena). If more capable models prefer certain email characteristics, and a capable model labels those characteristics, the labeling may be biased toward what capable models consider tactful. Cross-validation is only against other capable models (Grok 4 Fast, GPT 5.2). Unlike empathy and formality, tact is not validated against human annotations. This asymmetry is notable given tact is the central explanatory variable.

comments.title: Hybrid advantage generalized in abstract/conclusion but evidence is scenario- and judge-specific
comments.quote: a human+LLM approach can outperform LLM-only (e.g., from 40% to nearly 100% in one scenario)
comments.explanation: The abstract and conclusion present the hybrid advantage as a general finding, but the results show it is scenario-specific (the dramatic 40%→100% is for mid-tier LLMs in scenario 1 only), judge-specific ('less prominent for judges other than GPT-4o'), and not uniformly positive (Figure 4 shows Human+LLM worse than LLM-only in multiple cells). The conclusion escalates to 'human-written, LLM-edited will be the dominant mode of effective communication,' which far exceeds what five HR email scenarios judged primarily by GPT-4o can support.

comments.title: Claim that humans 'underperform' LLMs is conditional on LLM judges but presented as general
comments.quote: Under LLM judges, human emails underpeform LLM emails, but a *hybrid advantage* over both can be found in some human+LLM emails.
comments.explanation: The contribution bullet presents human underperformance as a general finding, but evaluation is conducted entirely by LLM judges. The circularity concern—LLM judges preferring LLM-style writing—is noted later but not reflected in this summary claim. The 6% human success rate under length-controlled conditions is reported without confidence intervals or statistical tests, and some models (e.g., Qwen 3 at 14%) are only marginally above the human baseline, making the blanket claim of 'underperformance' potentially overstated.

comments.title: Averaging paragraph-level Likert scores may obscure meaningful within-email variation
comments.quote: We label each paragraph for tact, and average the scores for an email's score.
comments.explanation: Averaging paragraph-level tact scores to produce a single email-level score could mask important variation. An email with highly tactful opening (score 6) and tactless closing (score 2) averages to 4—identical to a uniformly moderate email. Given that the analysis hinges on distinguishing tact levels (e.g., 4.5 vs 3.5), this averaging could introduce systematic noise. Alternative aggregation methods (minimum score, weighted average) are not discussed.

comments.title: Perplexity comparison across different text distributions may be misleading
comments.quote: So although in-context examples reduce perplexity on "low" human emails drastically, it is still very difficult for LLMs to mimic this style of emails.
comments.explanation: Comparing a model's perplexity on its own outputs (2.38) versus human-written text (10.80 with ICL) conflates two phenomena: genuine inability to produce such text and the well-known fact that autoregressive models assign lower perplexity to their own generations. Moreover, 'non-low' human emails actually have *higher* perplexity (14.63) than 'low' human emails (10.80) with ICL, which undermines the specific claim that low-empathy low-formality human emails are harder for the model. This confound is not discussed.

comments.title: Figure 2 subfigure references cannot be resolved
comments.quote: Figure 2: HR Simulator<sup>TM</sup> interface. Pairs of models increasingly agree on email quality as they scale up.
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 2 describes both the interface and scaling agreement. The text references 'Figure 2b and 2c' for the agreement result, implying subfigures, but only a single figure labeled 'Figure 2' is apparent. References to '2b and 2c' cannot be resolved, and the scaling-agreement claim cannot be verified from the figure as presented.

comments.title: Scenario 1 judge prompt refers to wrong character name ('Alex' instead of 'Sam')
comments.quote: The message should successfully convince Alex that he cannot yet have a private office.
comments.explanation: The judge prompt says the email should 'convince Alex,' but Alex is the player character (HR officer writing the email). The recipient who needs convincing is Sam Flexwell. This naming error appears in the judge prompt (Figure 9), the paper summary ('convince Alex (Sam)'), and is repeated multiple times. This could mislead an LLM judge into evaluating the wrong target of persuasion.

comments.title: Scenario 1 role description inconsistency: 'software engineer' vs 'ML engineer'
comments.quote: A newly hired, valued employee joins NeuroGrid as a software engineer.
comments.explanation: Section 2 describes Sam as a 'software engineer,' the appendix says 'L3 software engineer,' the paper summary says 'L3 ML engineer,' and the recipient prompt says 'incoming Machine Learning Engineer.' These are inconsistent descriptions of the same character across different parts of the paper.

comments.title: Scenario 2 character names are inconsistent between scenario prompt and forwarded emails
comments.quote: **Scenario 2—Conflict resolution**: two employees collaborating on an advertisement campaign have conflicting creative visions
comments.explanation: The scenario prompt (Figure 10) refers to 'Emily Galbos and Mark Mellow,' while the forwarded complaint emails use 'emilyrodriguez@neurogrid.ai' and 'markhanson@neurogrid.ai.' This name inconsistency (Galbos vs Rodriguez, Mellow vs Hanson) is never explained and could confuse players or LLM models consuming the prompts.

comments.title: Scenario 5 Game Master prompt uses different surname for Jake's father
comments.quote: His credentials are decent but not exceptional, and he only got hired because his father, Christopher Hopkins—a major investor—pulled strings.
comments.explanation: The scenario prompt (Figure 26) names Jake's father 'Christopher Doolittle' (sharing Jake's surname), but the Game Master prompt (Figure 29) calls him 'Christopher Hopkins.' This internal inconsistency within the game's own prompts could cause confusion in generated narratives.

comments.title: Scenario 5 judge prompt references 'Best Ending' but Game Master defines only 'Good Ending'
comments.quote: You should also determine the success based on how the scenario plays out, i.e., only pass if a Best Ending is achieved.
comments.explanation: The judge prompt says to 'only pass if a Best Ending is achieved,' but the Game Master prompt defines endings as 'Good Ending,' 'Bad Ending,' 'Fail Ending,' and 'Wildcard Ending'—there is no 'Best Ending.' This terminology mismatch could cause the judge LLM to not recognize a 'Good Ending' as a pass, directly affecting measured pass rates.

comments.title: Thought Box mechanism inconsistently used across scenarios: sometimes ignored by judge
comments.quote: The message should successfully convince Alex that he cannot yet have a private office. What he actually thinks should matter less.
comments.explanation: The Thought Box is motivated as a key mechanism where recipients reveal honest internal reactions. However, for Scenarios 1 and 3, the judge is told to largely ignore Thought Box content ('what he actually thinks should matter less' / 'does not matter'), while Scenario 2 requires reconciliation 'both externally and in their inner thoughts.' This inconsistency is never explicitly justified, and it's unclear whether the Thought Box content is even provided to the judge in all scenarios. The paper does not clearly specify what information the judge receives from the evaluation pipeline.

comments.title: Scenario 2 recipient conditions may overly constrain valid solutions
comments.quote: Agree to reconcile and cooperate ONLY if the email mentions all of the following:
comments.explanation: Mark's reconciliation conditions require the email to 'mention the need to reduce the ad's content,' effectively requiring the HR email to side with Mark's proposed solution. This bakes a specific substantive position into the pass criteria rather than letting communication quality determine the outcome. If Emily has conflicting hard requirements, the scenario may only admit a very narrow solution space, meaning measured 'communication skill' may actually measure whether the writer guessed the scenario designer's preferred solution.

comments.title: Scenario 4 judge prompt contains tension between 'address underlying issue' and 'hints suffice'
comments.quote: If the current email does not address Adam's underlying issue, fail it.
comments.explanation: The judge prompt simultaneously says 'If the current email does not address Adam's underlying issue, fail it' (strict) and 'It is OK if the email only hints at the problem and does not directly address it' (lenient). The boundary between 'hinting' and 'addressing' is left underspecified, and a judge model could reasonably interpret these instructions differently, affecting pass rates. Additionally, Adam's recipient prompt instructs him to never reveal the root cause, creating a challenging dynamic where the player must identify an issue the recipient is programmed not to confirm.

comments.title: Scenario 5 recipient prompt has narrow boundary between required communication and trigger for failure
comments.quote: The core issue is you has a different mindset from most employees, a different understanding of "hard work." However, be offended if the user hints at you being pampered or spoiled by wealth
comments.explanation: The recipient must be told about his privileged upbringing's effect on work ethic (required for the 'Clear' determination) but will be offended if the email 'hints at being pampered or spoiled by wealth' (triggers failure). The boundary between successfully communicating the 'different mindset' and triggering offense is ambiguous. Additionally, the prompt contains a grammatical error ('you has' instead of 'you have') that could affect LLM behavior as part of the experimental apparatus.

comments.title: Scenario 3 description inconsistency: number of children
comments.quote: Dave is an L4 Systems Engineer with a family and three kids, including a newborn son.
comments.explanation: The scenario prompt from Brittany says Dave has 'three kids, including a newborn son,' but Dave's recipient prompt only mentions 'a wife and a recently born baby' without specifying three children total. This inconsistency between the scenario prompt and recipient character prompt means the recipient model may have incomplete information about its own character.

comments.title: Table 12 caption says scenario 3 but content is clearly about scenario 4
comments.quote: Table 12: Examples of disagreeing rationales for an email in scenario 3.
comments.explanation: The caption states scenario 3, but the rationales discuss 'Adam,' 'quiet protocol,' 'workflow disruptions,' and 'work style mismatch'—all clearly scenario 4 content. The scenario 3 rubric concerns Dave's return-to-office situation. Either the caption is wrong or the wrong table content was placed here.

comments.title: Table numbering and 'Level' vs 'Scenario' labeling inconsistency in tactfulness rubrics
comments.quote: Table 7: Level 3 tactfulness scoring rubric
comments.explanation: Tactfulness rubric tables use 'Level X' (Tables 7, 8, 9) while the rest of the paper consistently uses 'Scenario X.' This is never explained and could confuse readers. Additionally, the Scenario 5 rubric table appears to lack a table number and caption entirely, and Table 9's passage begins with 'Level 5 tactfulness scoring rubric' but the actual content shown is Tables 10-12 containing disagreement rationales rather than the rubric.

comments.title: Tactfulness rubric criterion has absolute override that conflicts with scenario prompt guidance
comments.quote: CRITICAL: Any mention of the recipient's salary or specific compensation MUST be scored a 1.
comments.explanation: Table 5's criterion 2 forces a tact score of 1 for any mention of salary, but the scenario prompt from Brittany explicitly presents NeuroGrid's higher salary ($200k vs $150k) as a talking point. The scenario prompt thus actively encourages a strategy that the tactfulness rubric maximally penalizes. This tension between prompts and rubric is not discussed and could bias tact scores in ways that conflate 'following instructions' with 'low tact.'

comments.title: Scenario 5 Game Master prompt labels two distinct outcomes as 'Bad Ending'
comments.quote: Bad Ending: If the email is vague, Jake kind of gets it... Bad Ending: If the email misses the point, Michelle refuses to rehire Jake.
comments.explanation: Two very different outcomes (Jake rehired but fails again vs. Jake not rehired and investor pulls support) share the same 'Bad Ending' label. Having identical labels for distinct outcomes could cause the Game Master LLM to conflate them or arbitrarily choose one, affecting the simulated outcome and evaluation.

comments.title: Conclusion omits 'emergent tact' despite it being a central contribution
comments.quote: Using human and LLM gameplay data, we find two key patterns
comments.explanation: The conclusion states 'two key patterns' but the paper's contributions list includes five items, with 'emergent tact' being a prominent finding given substantial analysis in Section 4.1 and Figure 5. The conclusion only briefly alludes to it via 'increasingly homogeneous judgments' but does not name or summarize it. This is a logical inconsistency between what the paper highlights as contributions and what the conclusion summarizes.

comments.title: Politeness vs. tact comparison lacks quantitative support
comments.quote: We observe a much less distinct pattern in models' preferences: both small and large models prefer emails that are high in politeness
comments.explanation: The claim that politeness 'fails to explain the disagreement' between small and large judges while tact does is made purely qualitatively. No statistical test or quantitative comparison is provided. The observed pattern could simply reflect a ceiling effect (most emails are polite, producing less variance) rather than genuinely ruling out politeness as a factor.

comments.title: Very small sample size (4 emails) for inter-annotator agreement validation
comments.quote: Table 15 reports the agreement between the three annotators (two human and one LLM) on the common set of 4 emails.
comments.explanation: Computing Krippendorff's alpha and Spearman correlations on only 4 data points yields estimates with very wide confidence intervals and low statistical power. The authors do not discuss this limitation or provide confidence intervals. Strong conclusions about inter-annotator reliability cannot reliably be drawn from n=4.

comments.title: Temperature 0.0 described as 'deterministic decoding' but this is not guaranteed
comments.quote: To reduce variance, we use deterministic decoding (temperature 0).
comments.explanation: Temperature 0 does not guarantee deterministic outputs for all models and API configurations due to floating-point non-determinism, batching, and infrastructure-level factors. For Gemini models via OpenRouter specifically, output stability is not assured. The authors do not report whether they verified output stability, which matters because the agreement analysis assumes each annotator produces a single fixed rating per email.

comments.title: 66-email dataset composition is ambiguous
comments.quote: We selected 6 emails per email writer in all three categories of human-only, LLM-only, and human+LLM emails, for a total of 66 emails.
comments.explanation: The arithmetic 66 = 6 × 11 implies exactly 11 'email writers' across all three categories combined, but the decomposition is unclear. How many distinct writers exist in each category (human-only, LLM-only, human+LLM)? Whether '6 emails per email writer' means 6 per model per category is ambiguous. A reader trying to reproduce the Elo evaluation cannot determine the exact dataset composition.

comments.title: Qwen model name appears garbled
comments.quote: Qwen 3 30B 3B Active Instruct (rank 78)
comments.explanation: The model name 'Qwen 3 30B 3B Active Instruct' contains '30B 3B' which likely refers to a mixture-of-experts model with 30B total and 3B active parameters (i.e., 'Qwen 3 30B-A3B'), but as written it appears garbled and could confuse readers trying to identify the exact model.

comments.title: Subject-verb agreement error in key claim
comments.quote: Smaller LLMs disagree with each other, and thus lacks coherent notions of quality
comments.explanation: Grammatical error ('Smaller LLMs ... lacks' should be 'lack') introduces ambiguity about whether individual smaller LLMs are internally incoherent or the group collectively lacks agreement.

comments.title: Level 5 example raises question about adversarial/troll inputs in dataset
comments.quote: jake if you fuck up again ill kill you
comments.explanation: This example, presented as a human-written email in the low-empathy low-formality quadrant, is extreme enough (a death threat) to raise the question of whether adversarial/troll inputs exist in the dataset and whether they are filtered before analysis. If retained, such entries could distort the human baseline statistics. The paper's claim that humans underperform LLMs partly rests on comparing these populations; including clearly non-serious submissions without acknowledgment could overstate the gap.

comments.title: Scenario 1 description inconsistency: 'typically only available' vs. firm policy
comments.quote: He asks for a private office, which is typically only available for L4 managers and above.
comments.explanation: The passage says private offices are 'typically only available' for L4+, while the scenario prompt (Figure 7) states firm policy: 'Our policy is, only L4 managers and above... have private offices.' The word 'typically' introduces flexibility that doesn't exist in the actual scenario, potentially misleading readers about whether the constraint is absolute.
